records.ety: [L. plaga a blow, stroke, plague; akin to Gr. <?/, fr. <?/ to strike; cf. L. plangere to strike, beat. Cf. Plaint.]

defs: That which smites, wounds, or troubles; a blow; a calamity; any afflictive evil or torment; a great trail or vexation.
quotes: And men blasphemed God for the plague of hail.
The different plague of each calamity.

defs: An acute malignant contagious fever, that often prevails in Egypt, Syria, and Turkey, and has at times visited the large cities of Europe with frightful mortality; hence, any pestilence; as, the great London plague.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Plagued (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Plaguing.]

defs: To infest or afflict with disease, calamity, or natural evil of any kind.
quotes: Thus were they plagued And worn with famine.

defs: Fig.: To vex; to tease; to harass.
quotes: She will plague the man that loves her most.
